title: stolen_api_keys
description: A developer accidentally commits an AWS access key to a public GitHub repository. Within minutes, the key is discovered and used to spin up crypto-mining infrastructure at the organisation's expense.

timeline.title: Exposed Key Discovered
timeline.description: GitHub's secret scanning and an internal alert both flag an AWS access key committed to a public repository 15 minutes ago.

timeline.title: Unauthorised Cloud Activity Detected
timeline.description: AWS CloudTrail shows the key was used to create 12 EC2 instances in an unfamiliar region within 3 minutes of the commit being pushed.

timeline.title: Crypto-Mining Operation Identified
timeline.description: The unauthorised EC2 instances are confirmed to be running crypto-mining software. Significant cost exposure is accumulating.

timeline.title: API Key Revoked
timeline.description: The exposed AWS access key is revoked in IAM. The rogue EC2 instances lose their authorisation source.

timeline.title: Cloud Environment Audited
timeline.description: Full IAM audit conducted. All rogue resources terminated and AWS support engaged regarding fraudulent charges.

timeline.title: Developer Security Training Initiated
timeline.description: Pre-commit secret scanning hooks and IAM role-based access deployed. Developer security awareness training scheduled.

node-1.title: AWS Key Exposed in Public Repository
node-1.situation: GitHub's secret scanning sends an alert: a developer pushed a commit to a public repository containing a live AWS IAM access key and secret. The commit has been public for approximately 15 minutes. Automated bots are known to scrape public repositories for secrets within seconds of publication.
node-1.question: What is your first action?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately revoke the exposed AWS access key in the IAM console — assume it has already been discovered.

options.label: B
options.text: Delete the commit from the repository and assume the key is safe since it was only public for 15 minutes.

options.label: C
options.text: Rotate the key only if CloudTrail shows evidence of unauthorised use.

node-2.title: Unauthorised EC2 Instances Detected
node-2.situation: AWS CloudTrail logs confirm the key was used within 3 minutes of the commit being pushed to create 12 EC2 instances in ap-southeast-1 — a region your organisation never uses. The key has now been revoked, but the instances are still running.
node-2.question: How do you respond?

options.label: A
options.text: Terminate all unauthorised EC2 instances, quarantine the IAM user, and audit all API activity that occurred since the key was first exposed.

options.label: B
options.text: Terminate the EC2 instances without reviewing other CloudTrail activity — the key is revoked so the threat is over.

node-3.title: Crypto-Mining Confirmed — Cost Exposure
node-3.situation: The unauthorised EC2 instances were running XMRig crypto-mining software. The AWS billing dashboard shows $4,200 in unexpected charges accrued in under two hours. The full audit of activity from the exposed key is still in progress.
node-3.question: How do you handle the financial impact?

options.label: A
options.text: Document the full incident timeline, open a support case with AWS to dispute the fraudulent charges, and review all IAM policies associated with the exposed key for excessive permissions.

options.label: B
options.text: Accept the charges as a cost of the incident and take no further action on the billing.

node-4.title: Preventing Future API Key Exposure
node-4.situation: AWS has acknowledged the fraudulent charge dispute. The full audit reveals no other data was accessed. Leadership wants assurance this type of incident cannot happen again.
node-4.question: What controls do you implement?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement pre-commit hooks and CI/CD secret scanning to block key commits, migrate from long-lived IAM access keys to short-lived IAM roles, and run mandatory developer security training on secrets management.

options.label: B
options.text: Only brief the developer who made the mistake and update the internal coding guidelines document.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: All rogue AWS resources have been terminated and the fraudulent charge dispute has been submitted to AWS. Pre-commit secret scanning hooks are in place across all repositories, IAM long-lived keys have been replaced with role-based access, and developer security training has been scheduled.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
